title: Versals: Built-Up Capitals
subtitle: Three-stroke build method for Roman versal capitals using Johnston's technique
excerpt: Versal capitals are large display letters built from multiple pen strokes rather than written in a single pass. Each stroke is narrow; the letter is assembled by placing two outer strokes and filling between them. The Johnston three-stroke build gives fine, controlled thick-thin contrast at any scale.
sourceNotes: Johnston, Edward. Writing and Illuminating and Lettering. London: Pitman, 1906. Built-up capitals, three-stroke build method.

glossaryTerms.definition: A large decorated or built-up initial capital letter used to mark the opening of a chapter, verse, or significant passage. Versals are constructed stroke by stroke rather than written in a single pass, which allows precise control of their proportions.

The enclosed or partially enclosed white space within a letterform, such as the inside of 'O' or the aperture of 'C'. In versal construction the counter must be protected by careful placement of the outer strokes.
Any letterform assembled from multiple pen strokes laid side by side and filled in, rather than written as a continuous movement. The opposite of a written or spontaneous letter.

content.text: is not written: it is built. Johnston's three-stroke method places two thin outer strokes to define the edges of a thick stroke, then fills between them. The result is a letter with the precision of an engraved or painted capital but made entirely with a standard broad-edge pen.

Use a Mitchell Roundhand No. 0, the broadest of the standard Mitchell nibs. Versals are typically written at a letter height of 10 to 15 nib widths; the broad nib means individual strokes are narrow relative to the finished letter, which is what allows fine built-up control. Unlike foundational hands where nib width determines stroke width, in versal work the stroke width is set by how far apart you place your two outer lines.
The left-edge stroke: pull the nib downward along the left edge of the intended thick stem. The stroke is a single nib-width line.
The right-edge stroke: pull the nib downward along the right edge of the intended thick stem, parallel to and the desired stem-width away from stroke 1.

The fill stroke: load the nib generously and drag it between the two edge strokes from top to bottom, filling the white space with ink. Work in overlapping passes from one edge to the other, using the edge strokes as guides.
Thin strokes (serifs, hair lines, and the thin parts of curved letters) are made with a single pulled nib stroke at the natural angle.

Versals are written with the nib held at approximately 30 degrees to the horizontal for the edge strokes. This is slightly steeper than uncial but shallower than foundational. The angle produces slightly wedge-shaped serifs that echo classical Roman carved inscriptions. For fill strokes, flatten the angle slightly so the nib sweeps smoothly across the full stem width.
The capital O is the key test letter for versals. Its
(the enclosed white space) should be a near-perfect ellipse that mirrors the outer shape.

attrs.definition: Three-stroke build for each half of the O. Left half: stroke 1 traces the outer left arc from top to bottom; stroke 2 traces the inner left arc parallel, defining the stem width; stroke 3 fills between them. Right half mirrors this with strokes 4, 5, 6. The counter must remain a clean, symmetrical ellipse. Nib angle 30 degrees for edge strokes. Letter height 12 to 15 nib widths.

content.text: Johnston versals use club serifs: the serif is a short, slightly clubbed horizontal stroke attached to the top and bottom of every upright stem. To make a club serif, hold the nib at its steepest (about 45 degrees), pull a very short stroke diagonally to the left to form the left foot, then rotate back to 30 degrees to continue into the stem. A matching stroke at the top closes the serif on the other side.

Johnston took versal proportions from Roman inscriptions, mainly the Trajan Column. Key widths: O is nearly circular. C and G are about three-quarters of O's width. W is the widest letter, roughly 1.4 times its own height. M has a pointed apex and splayed legs. Narrow letters (E, F, I, J, L) are about half their height in width.

items.symptom: Fill strokes show streaks and do not merge smoothly
items.cause: Ink too dry, or fill passes made too far apart.
items.fix: Load the nib generously before each fill pass. Work passes close together (overlapping by half a nib width) while the ink is still wet so they merge.

items.cause: The nib is being pushed sideways rather than pulled cleanly.
items.fix: Edge strokes must be pulled strokes. Turn the paper if necessary so that the stroke always travels toward you. Short strokes worked in sections are more controllable than one long stroke.

items.cause: Left and right arc strokes are not symmetric around the vertical centre line.
items.fix: Rule a vertical centre line lightly in pencil. Work each half of the curved letters by referring to this axis. Mark the widest point of the counter before beginning.
